issue_title: conda skeleton pypi should use pip to build/install
issue_body:
conda skeleton creates build  scripts that do:

setup.py install

which is the "old" way to install python packages. It has the advantage of working with either setuptool sor raw distutils, but it has the disadvantage of not creating the proper pip meta-data, and sometimes using easy-install under the hood to build and install dependencies.

The "Modern" way to do it is with pip:

pip install ./

pip will monkey-patch setuptools in if necessary and create the proper pip meta-data.

(we probably want --no-deps, too, and maybe another flag or two.)

issue_title: Conda installs dependencies from the wrong channel
issue_body:
I'm using two channels, like this (`~/.condarc`):
```
channels:
  - defaults
  - conda-forge
```
so I expect packages to be pulled from `defaults`, if available, or from `conda-forge` otherwise. I explicitly installed a package from `conda-forge`, like this:
```
conda install gmt -c conda-forge/label/dev
```
I don't think this package is getting updated automatically with `conda update --all` (as newers versions are not in the `defaults` channel), so I manually gave the install command once again, to force an update:
```
conda install gmt -c conda-forge/label/dev
```
Turns out that no new version is available, but conda still tries to install two packages, replacing the currently installed version (from `defaults`) with versions from `conda-forge`:
```
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /home/pl/miniconda3/envs/sismo

  added / updated specs:
    - gmt


The following packages will be downloaded:

    package                    |            build
    ---------------------------|-----------------
    numpy-1.14.0rc1            |py36_blas_openblas_200         9.0 MB  conda-forge/label/dev
    scipy-1.0.0                |py36_blas_openblas_201        40.3 MB  conda-forge
    ------------------------------------------------------------
                                           Total:        49.3 MB

The following packages will be UPDATED:

    scipy: 1.0.0-py36hbf646e7_0  --> 1.0.0-py36_blas_openblas_201     conda-forge           [blas_openblas]

The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:

    numpy: 1.14.2-py36hdbf6ddf_0 --> 1.14.0rc1-py36_blas_openblas_200 conda-forge/label/dev [blas_openblas]

Proceed ([y]/n)?
```
I think this is a bug.

issue_title: NEw SCOUT: not possible to choose a gen list in the filter function.
Segmentation fault after processing data in a different process
issue_body:
I have the following issue and do not know if it is my fault or an error in pysam:

I read the data from a samfile, store it in a buffer and then I hand over this buffer to a new Process of the multiprocessing library in python. Inside this new process I can access the data without any issue but if I return via a Queue a new buffer which contains elements of the old buffer if some requirements are fullfilled, I get a segmentation fault. 

I will show you a code example and I hope you can understand better what is going on.

```python

import pysam
import multiprocessing

def readData(pFileOneIterator, pFileTwoIterator, pNumberOfItemsPerBuffer):
   j = 0
   buffer_1 = []
   buffer_2 = []
   while j < pNumberOfItemsPerBuffer:
        j += 1
        try:
            mate1 = pFileOneIterator.next()
            mate2 = pFileTwoIterator.next()
        except StopIteration:
            break
        # if some_conditions on mate1 and mate2 fit
        buffer_1.append(mate1)
        buffer_2.append(mate2)
    return buffer_1, buffer_2

def process_data(pMateBuffer1, pMateBuffer2, pQueue):
    # process data
    i = 0
    buffer_out = []
    while i < len(pMateBuffer1):
        mate1 = pMateBuffer1[i]
        mate2 = pMateBuffer2[i]

        # if some conditions are fulfilled, add 
        buffer_out.append(mate1)
        buffer_out.append(mate2)
   # this access works
   print buffer_out[0].flag
   pQueue.put(buffer_out)
  
def main(args=None):
    # create alignment file
    str1 = pysam.Samfile('File1', 'rb')
    str2 = pysam.Samfile('File2', 'rb')

    buffer_1, buffer_2 = readData(str1, str2, 500000)
    queue = multiprocessing.Queue()
    p = multiprocessing.Process(target=process_data, kwargs=dict(
                    pMateBuffer1=buffer_1,
                    pMateBuffer2=buffer_2,
                    pQueue=queue
                    ))
    p.start()
    # let process compute and then get data back
    result = queue.get()
    # type can be seen:
    print type(result[0]) # prints <type 'pysam.libcalignedsegment.AlignedSegment'>
    # but this access raises a segementation fault
    print result[0].flag # Segmentation fault (core dumped)
    p.join()
```
Why can I copy the data from process A to B, but for some reasons it fails to copy all the data back from B to A? Am I doing something wrong if yes, what? For me it looks like that some pointers or references in the underlying data structure are deleted. 

Thanks a lot for your help!

issue_title: PyCharm and Mypy cannot find pysam classes
issue_body:
I am using PyCharm for development and mypy for static type checking, and unfortunately they are not able to find the some of the core pysam classes, for example `pysam.AlignmentFile`:

```
class SamFile(pysam.AlignmentFile):
    pass
```

Trying to figure out why, I found that `__al_l_` is not only defined in `pysam.__init__.py`, but that `__all__` imports the classes to make public from each of the `libc*` files, for example `libcalignmentfile.__all__`.  I believe that `libcalignmentfile.pyx`, where `libcalignmentfile.__all__` is defined, gets compiled to C, and so the IDE cannot find the definition.  Perhaps I am missing something (I often do).

It would really help developers who use pysam to be able to use PyCharm and mypy with pysam.  Thanks for any help you can offer in advance.

issue_title: Convert create commands to dynamic template generation
issue_body: In order to support Profiles, some `create` commands need to be converted to use either SDK calls or dynamic template generation.

**Describe the bug**
I had a problem where `az acr build` would always send an empty context (75 bytes, and the `.tar.gz` file in `%TEMP%` was empty).
Turns out my `.dockerignore` was the problem, I've attached it to this issue so you can take a look at it, but it basically ignores everything `**` then un-ignores a few things.
[dockerignore.zip](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/files/2233024/dockerignore.zip)

**To Reproduce**
- Create a `.dockerignore` file that starts with `**` then add a few `!` lines to un-ignore the folders you want to add to the context.

Confirmed with ARM team, that it is a known server bug
Will close after the fix is deployed

Currently  no unit tests of `msrest/paging` in this repo (everything on Autorest side).

We should have unit tests here, because we always need full testing. Moreover, it took me a while to understand a bug in #5  because of that.

Dear community,

We are currently working on splitting the library into different services, thus 4 new packages:
- azure-storage-blob
- azure-storage-file
- azure-storage-queue
- azure-storage-table

The split allows users to install only the service that they need. Anything that was previously directly under `azure.storage` are now in the new package `azure-storage-common`, which is a requirement for the new packages mentioned above. For example, `azure.storage.CloudStorageAccount` becomes `azure.storage.common.CloudStorageAccount`. The current `azure-storage` meta-package will be deprecated after this change.

The change is sitting on this [branch](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/tree/split-library), please take a look if you are interested. Any feedback is welcome! 😄  **Note: the new packages are not published to pypi yet.**

You can do `python3 build_packages.py all` to have all the packages built and put under the directory ./dist. Please follow the readme for more detailed instructions.

https://github.com/awebdeveloper/pre-commit-prettier/

issue_title: Is it really a good idea for the imports list to be separate?
issue_body:
This is a consistent question I get when I demo peru for people. Why not put the import path for a module in the module's declaration? There are two decent reasons and one bad reason:

1. It's nice to be able to look in one place and see everything that peru is going to do when you sync.
2. Imports are not necessarily one-module-one-path. A module could be imported multiple times with different rules.
3. Bad reason: this is a holdover from when remote modules were more involved, with potentially their own imports, when some symmetry between the remote modules and the toplevel module made sense.

Allowing import paths as part of a module declaration definitely simplifies the hello world example. I could go either way on examples that are more complicated than that. I *really* want to avoid having two different ways to do the same thing, like allowing both an imports list and inline import paths. I think the biggest question for me right now is whether point (1) is really true, or whether I just think it's true because I'm used to it...

issue_title: ForeignKey transform doesn't work when the class is specified by a string
issue_body:
Pylint-django works as advertised on a field declared like this:

``` py
field = ForeignKey(TheModel)
```

But not on this equivalent field:

``` py
field = ForeignKey('appname.TheModel')
```

Prior to https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8-import-order/commit/95fe1b1f2ed1db6051acd8d1163dfb12a6b4784a and the 0.14.3 release, the following lines from [`pelican/__init__.py`](https://github.com/getpelican/pelican/blob/master/pelican/__init__.py#L15-L18) produced the desired result (i.e., passes `flake8` tests):
```python
# pelican.log has to be the first pelican module to be loaded
# because logging.setLoggerClass has to be called before logging.getLogger
from pelican.log import init  # noqa
from pelican import signals
[... other imports ...]
```

Post-0.14.3 release, Travis is now reporting a test failure in a [recent pull request](https://github.com/getpelican/pelican/pull/2246#issuecomment-341734308):
```traceback
pelican/__init__.py:18:1: I100 Import statements are in the wrong order. from pelican should be before from pelican.log
```

I'm guessing this change in behavior is an unintended side effect that should be addressed in a subsequent `flake8-import-order` release?

### Describe what you wanted to do

Serveral files from F-Droid:
am.zoom.mbrowser_12.apk
android.androidVNC_13.apk
android.game.prboom_31.apk
...


### Describe what actually happened

    self.data_size = len(self.data_off_obj.map_item)
```

issue_title: Unable to install certficiate to Apache
issue_body:
I tried to obtain and install a certificate for Apache today and while I was able to obtain one, I wasn't able to install it. Apart from the fact that the command in the beta invite e-mail was using auth which is afaik not installing the certificate and you can't simply say “okay now install it”.

Anyway I got the following error:

    Cannot find a cert or key directive in
    /files/etc/apache2/sites-available/ssl.conf/virtualhost.
    VirtualHost was not modified

And I'm wondering what exactly that means. Now looking at the [source code](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt-apache/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L203) it seems that `ssl.conf` already needs to contain `SSLCertificateFile` and `SSLCertificateKeyFile` which seem odd because if they are already there… I wouldn't need to install it.

Now for full disclosure, I already had tried to use SSL so the file already had `SSLCertificateFile` defined (but not the `SSLCertificateKeyFile`). So maybe that's the issue?

Running `letsencrypt renew` with a value for `configurator` set on the CLI or in a configfile leads to errors like this:
```
2016-03-15 13:12:28,832:WARNING:letsencrypt.cli:An error occured while parsing /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/example.com.conf. The error was Too many flags setting configurators/installers/authenticators 'apache' -> ''. Skipping the file.
```
This problem is also causing the errors in #2663, where `letsencrypt-nginx/tests/boulder-integration.sh` is calling `letsencrypt` with `--configurator nginx` which causes `_set_by_cli` to raise an exception when creating a new lineage.

We need unit tests for just about everything in this project.  I know this isn't quite as exciting of a task... and that is why I listed it last, but it would be a major help.

It is imperative that we thoroughly test modules so that we can identify fine-grained errors.  As @kuba pointed out, it will also help you become familiar with the codebase and how it works.  It can enable you to contribute meaningfully without having to read the code in its entirety.

(I am currently working on writing an integration testing suite that I hope can test 100s of example servers quickly, but this only detect end problems and won't provide information about the minor errors that may occur during the course of execution)

issue_title: Python 3.3 and 3.5 not tested under Travis
issue_body:
**Description of issue or feature request**:
Pull request #59 temporarily excluded testing under Python versions 3.3 and 3.5 due to issues with Travis. Although testing under Tox does cover all of our supported Python versions, it is best if continuous integration covered all supported environments.  As recommended in https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/pull/59#issuecomment-339089728, we should open a ticket to keep track of this issue.

**Current behavior**:
Travis runs our unit tests under Python versions 2.7, 3.4, and 3.6.

**Expected behavior**:
Travis should run our unit tests under Python versions 2.7, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, and 3.6.

    if until and res > until:

```

According to RFC5545 Section 3.3.10:
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5545#section-3.3.10

> If the "DTSTART" property is specified as a date with UTC time or a date with local time and time zone reference, then the UNTIL rule part MUST be specified as a date with UTC time.

issue_title: Can't json encode some validation errors, "unorderable types: str() < int()"
issue_body:
I'm using marshmallow with flask, which has a jsonify() function that sorts keys, and i'm using that to return the validation errors to the client. In some situations, the dicts with validation errors returned by marshmallow combine int and str keys, which results in a TypeError while attempting to serialize them.

Here's a somewhat minimized test case:

```python
import json
import marshmallow as ma

class RoleSchema(ma.Schema):
    name = ma.fields.Str()

class UserSchema(ma.Schema):
    roles = ma.fields.Nested(RoleSchema, many=True)

user, errors = UserSchema().load({'roles':['name']})
print(errors)
print(json.dumps(errors))
print(json.dumps(errors, sort_keys=True))
````

Output:

```
{'roles': {0: {}, '_schema': ['Invalid input type.']}}
{"roles": {"0": {}, "_schema": ["Invalid input type."]}}

    print(json.dumps(errors, sort_keys=True))

    **kw).encode(obj)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 199, in encode
    chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 257, in iterencode
    return _iterencode(o, 0)
TypeError: unorderable types: str() < int()
````

I think the relevant function is `ErrorStore.get_errors(index=0)` 

https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/2.9.0/marshmallow/marshalling.py#L46

Stringifying the indexes in that function fixes it for me. Not sure if this is the best way to fix it though.

```diff
--- a/marshalling.py
+++ b/marshalling.py
@@ -45,6 +45,7 @@

     def get_errors(self, index=None):
         if index is not None:
+            index = str(index)
             errors = self.errors.get(index, {})
             self.errors[index] = errors
         else:

````

issue_title: Unable to run geoserver and authentication error
issue_body:
I am very new to cartoview and geonode. I was trying  to access the geoserver through cartoview but was unable to so i installed geonode using the ppa: geonde/stable repository but now i am getting this error
![cartoviewerror](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/30353072/30214393-eea84d1a-94c9-11e7-8aa8-cfb91a8e64e1.png)

Please help me out.
@hishamkaram

I'm trying to modify the `type` of a `ResourceRelatedField`: Instead of the model name, i'd like to serve another name. 

For `GET` requests, that works nicely because `to_representation` considers `resource_name` on the serializer (if the field is part of `included_serializers`).

However, `POST`ing a new resource under the different `resource_name` does not work, because `to_internal_value` considers only the model for `expected_relation_type`.

**Suggestion**: Allow passing `resource_name` to `ResourceRelatedField`. If specified, it 

- is used in `to_internal_value` to map to the actual resource name (which is read from the queryset)
- should take priority over `resource_name` on the serializer in `to_representation`

What do you think? Is there an easier approach to this? I'd be happy to provide a PR if you think this is the way to go.

PS: In case you wonder, our use case for this: We need two different endpoints with different permissions and serializers for the same underlying Django model. (The "owner" of the model sees more than anyone else.)

issue_title: Error pointer always refers to attributes
Relationships with ViewSets showing other keys than "type" and "id"

issue_body:
After apply #18, I still can't use a RelationField.

```python
class Design(orm.ApiModel):
    class Meta:
        type = 'designs'
        api = api

    name = orm.AttributeField('name')
    sub_designs = orm.RelationField('sub_designs')

design  = Design.from_id(1)
print(design.name)

>>> AttributeError: 'JsonApiObject' object has no attribute 'name'
```

Commenting out the sub_designs line, I can print out the design name as expected.

issue_title: Non-termination when use Z3-opt(OCaml version) to solve a sample SMT2 file
issue_body:
In my project, I need to solve such a planning problem using Z3-opt(Ocaml version). The sample file(test.smt2) is as follows:

(declare-fun tmp0 () Int)
(declare-fun c1 () Int)
(declare-fun p0 () Int)
(declare-fun tmp1 () Int)
(declare-fun c0 () Int)
(assert (let ((a!1 (and 
                (= (to_real (- p0 c0)) 0.0)
                (= (to_real tmp1) 1.0)))
      (a!2 (not (= (to_real (- p0 c0)) 0.0)))
      (a!4 (and (= (to_real (- p0 c1)) 0.0)
                (= (to_real tmp0) 1.0)
                ))
      (a!5 (not (= (to_real (- p0 c1)) 0.0))))
(let ((a!3 (or a!1 (and a!2 (= (to_real tmp1) 0.0))))
      (a!6 (or a!4 (and a!5 (= (to_real tmp0) 0.0)))))
  (and a!3
       (= (to_real c1) (+ (to_real c0) 1.0))
       a!6))))
(maximize c1)
(check-sat)

When I run the Z3 on it using the command "./z3 test.smt2" in my computer, It seems that it doesn't return any result. It  just hangs on and doesn't terminate for at least 5 mins. The Z3 source file is downloaded from "https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3". Also I have tried the binary file of Z3. The Z3 binary file is download from "https://github.com/Z3Prover/bin/blob/master/nightly/z3-4.4.2.89fb5a44fba8-x64-ubuntu-14.04.zip". 
What's interesting, when I give  the SMT-input to the rise4fun in "http://rise4fun.com/Z3Opt", it could give the correct result quickly. 
Could you tell me how to solve this problem? Thank you!

issue_title: Z3 incompleteness example for non-matching types?
issue_body:
Hi everyone!

Just came across an example which seems to be "difficult" for Z3 to handle:

The following returns unknown:
```
(declare-const i_0 Real)
(declare-const i_1 Real)

(declare-const k_0 Real)

(declare-const j_0 String)
(declare-const j_1 String)

(assert (and
          (>= i_0 0.0)
          (= i_1 (* i_0 k_0))

	  (= "0" j_0)
          (= j_0 j_1)
        )
)

(assert (not
          (and
            (>= i_1 0.0)
            (= "0" j_1)
          )
        )
)

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

This also returns unknown:
```
(declare-const i_0 Real)
(declare-const i_1 Real)

(declare-const k_0 Real)

(declare-const j_0 Int)
(declare-const j_1 Int)

(assert (and
          (>= i_0 0.0)
          (= i_1 (* i_0 k_0))

	  (= 0 j_0)
          (= j_0 j_1)
        )
)

(assert (not
          (and
            (>= i_1 0.0)
            (= 0 j_1)
          )
        )
)

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

While this returns sat:
```
(declare-const i_0 Real)
(declare-const i_1 Real)

(declare-const k_0 Real)

(declare-const j_0 Real)
(declare-const j_1 Real)

(assert (and
          (>= i_0 0.0)
          (= i_1 (* i_0 k_0))

	  (= 0 j_0)
          (= j_0 j_1)
        )
)

(assert (not
          (and
            (>= i_1 0.0)
            (= 0 j_1)
          )
        )
)

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
With model:
```
i_0: 1.0
i_1: - 1.0
k_0: - 1.0
j_0: 0.0
j_1: 0.0
```

The differences between the examples is the type of `j_*` which is either String, Int or Real. Only when `j_*` matches the type of `i_*` as a Real is it able to find the satisfying model. To me this is confusing as the variables for `i_*` and `j_*`  do not share any relation and the model only changes in type and not value.

I strongly suspect this is a bug, hence the report. If I am doing something wrong, please tell me!

I found an insufficient synchronization that may cause data race in the following code.

https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/c64d044e60684aa54c8c4d5cc4d81460707bc65d/src/util/gparams.cpp#L440-L448

When the above function returns, the local variable "params_ref result" will be destructed. Thus, if there are two threads calling the function and returning at the same time, the destructor of params_ref, i.e., ~params_ref(), will be called at the same time in the two threads. The code of the destructor is as following:

https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/c64d044e60684aa54c8c4d5cc4d81460707bc65d/src/util/params.cpp#L507-L510

Line 509 will be called at the same time, which will crash the program.

issue_title: Specify output height/width directly in TransformerLayer (instead of scaling factor)
issue_body:
I think it would be more natural to specify the output width and height of a `TransformerLayer` directly, rather than specifying a downsampling factor. This is especially the case when the desired layer output has a different aspect ratio than the input. I realise that you can specify a tuple of two downsample factors for this case, but still, it seems a lot more straightforward to just specify the size in pixels directly.

A quick scan through the code shows that `downsample_factor` is only ever used to compute the output shape from the input shape anyway, so it doesn't seem like this makes any other part of the implementation more straightforward.

Was there any particular motivation to go with the scaling factor approach? If not, I think we should probably refactor the code to at least allow both approaches. Thoughts?

issue_title: The value of DateTimeField which enables 'auto_now' option can't be set from imported data
issue_body:
Thank you for providing this great software!
I found a problem about import processing, so I report it.

## Abstract
After import data, the current time is always set to the DateTimeField which is enabled 'auto_now'. On the other hands, DataTimeField which is disabled 'auto_now' could be set the time which is specified in the imported data, as following.

### Definition of Model
```python
class Hoge(models.Model):
    datetime1 = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=False)
    datetime2 = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
```
### Definition of ModelResource
```python
class HogeResource(ModelResource):
    class Meta:
        model = Hoge
        fields = ('id', 'datetime1', 'datetime2')
```
### Result of reproduction
```
$ python manage.py shell
Python 2.7.12 (default, Nov 19 2016, 06:48:10) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
(InteractiveConsole)
>>> import tablib
>>> from import_export import resources
>>> from hoge.admin import HogeResource
>>> from hoge.models import Hoge
>>> 
>>> raw_data = {'id': 1, 'datetime1': '2017-01-01 00:00:00', 'datetime2': '2017-01-01 00:00:00'}
>>> headers = ['id', 'datetime1', 'datetime2']
>>> 
>>> # clear data before testing
>>> Hoge.objects.all().delete()
(1, {u'hoge.Hoge': 1})
>>> 
>>> resource = HogeResource()
>>> dataset = tablib.Dataset([raw_data[x] for x in headers], headers=headers)
>>> result = resource.import_data(dataset)
>>> 
>>> obj = Hoge.objects.get(id=1)
>>> obj.datetime1
datetime.datetime(2017, 1, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>)
>>> obj.datetime2
datetime.datetime(2017, 8, 1, 2, 51, 53, 964519, tzinfo=<UTC>)
>>>
```
## Environment
I checked this problem was occur in the following environments.

|Relevant software|Version|
|:------------------|:-------:|
|django-import-export|0.5.2.dev0|
|django|1.11.3|
|tablib|0.11.5|
|Python|2.7.12, 3.5.2|

At the moment `import_data()` is a monolithic function with no easy customization points. In order to provide better feedback to users, I would like to extend it to report the total number of rows in each result type (NEW, UPDATE, SKIP, DELETE), including the total number of skipped rows even when `report_skipped == False`

There is no easy way to do this without copying the whole `import_data()` function into the subclass because there is no way to override Result class used, or the handling of individual rows.

I could achieve my requirements by making a slight change to import_data() to count the total number of each result type.

If it is desirable, I could also:

* replace the `result = Result()` with `result = self.get_result_class()()` to allow people to customize the Result if necessary
* move the inner part of the `for row in dataset.dict:` to a new `import_row()` method that would allow people to customize what happens before and after the row import is attempted
* add a `report_error()` and a `report_row_result()` to hold the code that appends the results to the result, once again providing an additional extension point for people that need additional reporting

issue_title: Q: timestamps from inconsistent clocks (microcontroller? or camera?)
issue_body:
Hey guys, 

we are recently grabbing images (rgb, depth and fisheye), IMU readings and the relevant timestamps from the ``ZR300`` sensor. We have correctly enabled the ``enable_motion_tracking`` but the timestamps for the rgb and depth seem from the camera clock instead of the motion microcontroller. This situation can be solved by increasing their frame rate to >60 but this problem remains if we drop the frame rate back to 30.

It is pretty easy to reproduce this problem. You could run the official example "cpp-motion-module" by changing the rgb and depth frame rate (around ``line 89-93``) from 60 to 30.

I am wondering what's the problem here? how can we keep getting the timestamps consistently from motion microcontroller for all the steams (at lower frame rate).

Posting here for feedback/discussion.

## Summary

It would be useful to be able to:

* Make it easy (via a mongo query) to see what volumetric data is available for a given task doc

* Optionally, store the volumetric data to make it easy to retrieve via a mongo query

The benefits of this are mainly:

* Easier aggregation and building (particularly for bader and other topological analysis, also for  processing of locpots)

* We're looking into charge density visualization in the future, and need to be able to easily retrieve chgcars to be able to do this

## Approaches

Two ideas:

1. In the Atomate Drone, in each `calcs_reversed`, store a list of available volumetric data files. In `insert_task`, we then optionally look up the files requested by the user and insert these into gridfs, e.g. `insert_task(store_volumetric=('chgcar', 'locpot'))`.

or

2. In each `calcs_reversed`, we store the full volumetric data file as a string when generating the taskdoc, e.g. `calcs_reversed[0]['volumetric_data']['chgcar'] = chgcar_string`. However, in `insert_task`, we choose whether to keep this string and store it via insertion into gridfs, or whether to just discard the string from the taskdoc and not store it at all.

Discussing with @shyamd, we were perhaps erring towards option 2, but I thought it'd be useful to open this to feedback from others. The problem with 2 is that it involves storing a lot of data in the taskdoc, even if temporarily, but this is basically what we do with bandstructure and dos objects so it seemed logically cleaner.

I have a draft implementation for option 1 available (https://github.com/hackingmaterials/atomate/compare/master...mkhorton:chgcar, changes to `VaspDrone` have been tested, changes to `insert_task` have not) to give an idea for what this might look like -- I think the changes to `VaspDrone` seem reasonable?

issue_title: Spinel: Revisit handling of FCS in SPINEL_PROP_STREAM_RAW
issue_body:
I think we need to revisit how the FCS(Final CheckSum) is currently handled for `SPINEL_PROP_STREAM_RAW`.

Currently, The NCP *crops* the FCS from the packet if it isn't provided and indicates this condition with a flag in the metadata. If the radio actually supports FCS, then the FCS will only be present for inbound frames, not outbound frames. This means that outbound frames are actually two bytes shorter than inbound frames in that case.

`wpantund` handles this (and other things, like RSSI, etc) by using the [PPI DLT](http://www.cacetech.com/documents/PPI%20Header%20format%201.0.7.pdf), which allows you to both specify the DLT per packet as well as append additional metadata like RSSI, etc.

But I think I'd rather move to an approach where simply interpreting the packet and ignoring the metadata "just works". Sure, the CRCs will read as zero (and, thus, as an error) when they aren't set, but lots of sniffer implementations do that for outbound packets—and it's better than (effectively) corrupting the packet by interpreting the last two non-FCS bytes of the frame as an actual FCS.

issue_title: analysis: Thread1.1 5.1.7 Minimum Supported Children – IPv6 Datagram Buffering
issue_body:
According to Conformance Document requirement for Router device:

- It should support minimal 10 children (at least 6 SEDs)

- Thread Router must be able to buffer at least one 1280-octet IPv6 datagram destined for an attached SED

- Thread Router must be able to buffer at least one 106-octet IPv6 datagram per attached SED

Test case 5.1.7 assumes that there are 12 thread devices in network (1 Leader + 1 DUT as Router + 10 children: 4ED and 6 SED). Currently we support maximum 5 children by default and allocate 64 message buffers totally, 128bytes size per buffer. For supporting 10 children, maybe we need more memory allocation for messages of buffering above IPv6 datagram.

issue_title: Numpy arrays ctors and flags rework
issue_body:
@wjakob This is a discussion / brainstorming issue for flags-related stuff in numpy api. Here's an unordered collection of my thoughts about it, please feel free to comment:

- `NPY_ARRAY_FORCECAST` by default. NumPy will then happily convert anything to anything even if it doesn't make any sense (this triggers unsafe casting mode), which doesn't play well for either input arguments or return values and quite often yields surprising results.
- The default flags combination in NumPy is `NPY_ARRAY_DEFAULT` which is comprised of:
  - `NPY_ARRAY_C_CONTIGUOUS`
  - `NPY_ARRAY_WRITEABLE`
  - `NPY_ARRAY_ALIGNED`
- NumPy also defines `NPY_ARRAY_OUT_ARRAY` which is the same as `NPY_ARRAY_DEFAULT`, and `NPY_ARRAY_IN_ARRAY` which is the same thing but without the "writeable" bit. If you think about it, most of the times the input arguments should not require writeability unless the purpose is to mutate them. It would be nice to be able to easily specify that.
- Constructor of `array_t` calls `PyArray_FromAny`, which is a universal conversion function "from anything". While its nice on its own and it would be beneficial to expose it separately (e.g. "::from_object()" static method), I believe it shouldn't be called in the ctor. Instead, it should check that the object is already an array (`PyArray_Check`) and then call array conversion routine (`PyArray_FromArray`) which also benefits from checking the casting rules (only two available: safe / force).
- It would be nice to have an `::astype(dtype, casting = safe) -> array` method on the `array` and also `::astype<T>(casting = safe) -> array_t<T>` method.
- It's currently impossible to specify flags for `array` whereas it may sometimes be beneficial (at least controlling the writeability). Obviously, `forcecast` flags doesn't make sense here, but numpy handles it the same way, some routines ignore some flags. This would mean that the ctor of `array` would be almost the same of that for `array_t`, calling `PyArray_CheckAny` and then `PyArray_FromArray`.
- Would it make sense to add an `ensurecopy` flag? When would it be used? (in light of the coming changes that would allow to provide an owner for the array so data is not copied)
- API-wise, it would be nice to be able to easily specify input/output arrays without fussing with type parameters. Not sure about it at all yet, but maybe some type aliases like `array::in(...)` or `array_t<int>::out(...)` or `array_t<int, array::forcecast>::out` that would set the proper set of flags.

Regards Falk
It could be `/etc/letsencrypt/hooks.d/pre/`, `/etc/letsencrypt/hooks.d/deploy/` (`--renew-hook` is likely to become `--deploy-hook`), `/etc/letsencrypt/hooks.d/auth/`, etc.

issue_title: Set from_block for event filters fromAPI
issue_body:
Currently the event filters from the API set `from_block=0` and `to_block='latest'` when no explicit values are given.

We should set better values for the `from_block`, probably the creation block of the `TokenNetwork[Registry]` that is queried.

`MatrixTransport::_send_queued_messages()` is supposed to send queued messages on startup, but since contents of the queue are raiden events (`raiden.transfer.mediated_transfer.events`) and they are transformed directly to a `raiden.message` without being signed, the signature in an outgoing message is set to `0x`.
As a result, partner will refuse to process such message.

## Problem Definition

All of our endpoints are using plural nouns since they are collections of resources. We need to make sure that `connection` endpoint follows this rule.



- [ ] Rename the connection endpoint
- [ ] Add a changelog entry.

issue_title: N^2 problem when registering custom classes.
issue_body:
Whenever a custom class is serialized by Ray for the first time on a worker (or driver), the class definition is pickled and shipped to all of the other workers (and the driver).

So if the same class definition is serialized on all of the workers, it will be exported to all of the other workers every single time, resulting in `N^2` messages, where `N` is the number of workers. This can be inspected as follows (on a single machine).

```python
import ray
import time

N = 10

ray.init(num_cpus=N)

class Foo(object):
    pass

@ray.remote
def f():
    time.sleep(0.1)
    return Foo()

for _ in range(N):
    f.remote()


# Then see how many things have been "exported".
exports = ray.worker.global_worker.redis_client.lrange('Exports', 0, -1)

print(len(exports))  # This should be something like N.
```

I have been reviewing the costs for hosting the nightly builds of streamlink on S3. For the month of April, `streamlink-lastest.exe` was downloaded 11,228 times and used 133GB of transfer bandwidth. The cost of transfers was $11.98 and the cost of storage and HTTP requests was $0.69, with a total cost of $12.67+tax. With the gaining popularity of `streamlink` I can only see that cost increasing, I would like to reduce the cost a bit ($15 a month is fairly expensive for me.) 

With the addition of `ffmpeg` we added some nice features, and the possibility for further future enhancements, but we also bloated the installer (and the git repo, as discussed on other issues.) The installer is approx. 38MB including the `ffmpeg.exe`, and approx. 15MB without `ffmpeg.exe`. 

I was thinking we could remove `ffmpeg` from the installer and either allow the user to install it themselves, or download the `ffmpeg.exe` on demand in the installer. I prefer the download during the installer option as it means the user doesn't have to install it manually but the installer is smaller (we can download `ffmpeg.exe` from github). 

Alternatively we could set up a different repo with scheduled builds on travis, to build the nightly periodically and automatically add a release to that repo. 

Thoughts @gravyboat, @bastimeyer, @cdrage?

One use-case is aggregating results of multiple checks in one.
Another is triggering a check on success/failure of another check.

To keep this archived somewhere, here are the notes from our discussion on dashboarding at the ipywidgets workshop in Paris in Jan 2018 (see https://github.com/OpenDreamKit/OpenDreamKit/issues/246, and https://www.lri.fr/etherpad/p/jupyter-widgets for the notes).

We can get an overview of current solutions to displaying the widgets on a web page, as sort of a web app. For example, Pascal's ipywidget-server, https://github.com/oschuett/appmode, or https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/pull/1862

Pascal's solution: a separate server that is instantiated with the code to be run, a specific kernel https://github.com/pbugnion/ipywidgets_server

https://github.com/oschuett/appmode - strip out the classic notebook UI

https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/pull/1862 (not finished) - read a notebook, execute all cells from the browser, display specific cell outputs

IBM Jupyter Dashboard server - easy drag-and-drop layout of cell outputs in a grid, one-click publish, secure execution of the notebook by not accepting code execution from the browser
https://github.com/jupyter-attic/dashboards_server and https://github.com/jupyter/dashboards  and https://github.com/jupyter-attic/dashboards_bundlers

Do we reuse kernels (e.g., you give a link to someone with a widget from a specific running kernel), or start a new kernel for *every* dashboard view

    - Different kinds of uses cases: dashboards as "isolated" kernel execution (each dashboard has own kernel) vs dashboard as "broadcast" enviornment - by interacting with one I am affecting all others.

Can we expose a layout widget that lets you drag and drop to layout things, and then save the layout.

Integration with kernel gateway / remote kernel manager?

It's not obvious we need a live separate kernel. For some usecases, you may want to execute the code in-process (e.g., flask, django).

Should the layout depend on widgets and the kernel? Or should the layout be independent of widgets/kernel.
  - Making two layout systems can be confusing (a frontend UI layout, and a widget layout)
  - We could make a layout widget that lets you drag and drop children to lay out them. Then save the state of the widget.

Do we force the authoring environment to be a notebook? An authoring environment like shiny? 

Perhaps we just have some very specific predefined layouts that can just be populated with children.

If we were able to publish a binder-ready repo for a dashboard, that could take care of a deployment usecase.

Needs:
    - Subject-matter experts (know python, not js) easily expose their code/explorations as a webapp to stakeholders with no programming skills, so they don't want any notion of a notebook, code, etc. Code might be running internal software stacks, etc., so we don't have to distribute software, just expose a GUI to use the tools.
    - Students exploring learning materials (similar to above usecase)
    - Should be usable from different language kernels, such as R
    - Layout should be doable programmatically
    - E-posters at an academic conference (three-column layout)
    - Include markdown/text/html, possibly with variable substitutions
    - Security - not being able to execute arbitrary code from the browser - needs to be an option
    - Programmatic layouts should use standard layout protocols, e.g., flexbox, grid, etc.
    - Other styling, such as colors, fonts, etc.
    -

issue_title: Gemm requires (MxK), (KxN) inputs but no reshape are inserted in onnx models.
issue_body:
I facing a problem when working with "bvlc_alexnet/model.pb" from [here](https://github.com/onnx/models/tree/master/bvlc_alexnet)

Its include a *Gemm* node with 3 inputs:
- The output of the previous node : pool_5, its shape is : [1, 256, 6, 6]
- A weights tensor presents into the initializers. its shape is : [4096, 9216]
- A bias tensor presents into initializers. its shape is : [4096]

The attrs of the node are : 
- Broadcasting = 1 : it's ok to broadcast the bias tensor.
- transB = 1 : it's ok to transpose the weights tensor.

In the [doc](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#gemm) we can read : 

> where input tensor A has dimension (M X K) , input tensor B has dimension (K X N), input tensor C and output tensor Y have dimension (M X N)

So the tensor B will have shape : [9216, 4096] after being transposed.
Si the input A (the output of the previous node) MUST have the shape : [1, 256\*6\*6] = [1, 9216]
This will produce a tensor of shape [1, 4096] which we can add to the bias tensor (input C).

According to me, a reshape is missing between the pool_5 node and the Gemm node.

- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/bwanamarko/sphinxcontrib-matlabdomain/issue/24

We recently added support for the `--age` flag to both `dstat` and `ddel`.

This has both semantic and performance value.
- Semantic: Most of the time, one is only interested in lookups on recent jobs.
- Performance: Lookups on labels for the Google provider can get very slow as your cloud project accumulates more and more operations.

The `--age` flag today is optional, meaning that by default all lookups are performed over all Google Genomics operations ever run within your cloud project. This is likely not what people want nearly all of the time, so it seems reasonable to restrict lookups by default to the last 30 days.

We'd also update `dstat` to emit its search criteria to `stderr` (like `ddel` does), adding the age restriction such that the functionality is transparent.
